metadata.summary: Jupyter core package. A base package on which Jupyter projects rely.

documentation: There is no reason to install this package on its own.

display environment information that is frequently

modules.name: jupyter_core.utils
documentation: Copyright (c) Jupyter Development Team.
Distributed under the terms of the Modified BSD License.

functions.name: jupyter_core.utils.ensure_dir_exists
documentation:
Ensure that a directory exists

If it doesn't exist, try to create it, protecting against a race condition
if another process is doing the same.
The default permissions are determined by the current umask.

functions.name: jupyter_core.utils._get_frame
documentation: Get the frame at the given stack level.

functions.name: jupyter_core.utils._external_stacklevel
documentation:
Find the stacklevel of the first frame that doesn't contain any of the given internal strings

The depth will be 1 at minimum in order to start checking at the caller of
the function that called this utility method.
This function is from https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/67998
(https://bugs.python.org/file39550/deprecated_module_stacklevel.diff) and
calculates the appropriate stacklevel for deprecations to target the
deprecation for the caller, no matter how many internal stack frames we have
added in the process. For example, with the deprecation warning in the
__init__ below, the appropriate stacklevel will change depending on how deep
the inheritance hierarchy is.

functions.name: jupyter_core.utils.deprecation
documentation:
Generate a deprecation warning targeting the first frame that is not 'internal'

internal is a string or list of strings, which if they appear in filenames in the
frames, the frames will be considered internal. Changing this can be useful if, for example,
we know that our internal code is calling out to another library.

functions.name: jupyter_core.utils.run_sync
documentation:
Wraps coroutine in a function that blocks until it has executed.

Parameters
----------
coro : coroutine-function
    The coroutine-function to be executed.

Returns
-------
result :
    Whatever the coroutine-function returns.

functions.name: jupyter_core.utils.ensure_async
documentation:
Convert a non-awaitable object to a coroutine if needed,
and await it if it was not already awaited.

This function is meant to be called on the result of calling a function,
when that function could either be asynchronous or not.

classes.name: jupyter_core.utils._TaskRunner

methods.name: jupyter_core.utils._TaskRunner.run
documentation: Synchronously run a coroutine on a background thread.
A task runner that runs an asyncio event loop on a background thread.

modules.name: jupyter_core.migrate
documentation:
Migrating IPython < 4.0 to Jupyter

This *copies* configuration and resources to their new locations in Jupyter

Migrations:

- .ipython/
  - nbextensions -> JUPYTER_DATA_DIR/nbextensions
  - kernels ->  JUPYTER_DATA_DIR/kernels

- .ipython/profile_default/
  - static/custom -> .jupyter/custom
  - nbconfig -> .jupyter/nbconfig
  - security/

    - notebook_secret, notebook_cookie_secret, nbsignatures.db -> JUPYTER_DATA_DIR

  - ipython_{notebook,nbconvert,qtconsole}_config.py -> .jupyter/jupyter_{name}_config.py
PYTHON_ARGCOMPLETE_OK

functions.name: jupyter_core.migrate.get_ipython_dir
documentation:
Return the IPython directory location.

Not imported from IPython because the IPython implementation
ensures that a writable directory exists,
creating a temporary directory if not.
We don't want to trigger that when checking if migration should happen.

We only need to support the IPython < 4 behavior for migration,
so importing for forward-compatibility and edge cases is not important.

functions.name: jupyter_core.migrate.migrate_dir
documentation: Migrate a directory from src to dst

functions.name: jupyter_core.migrate.migrate_file
documentation:
Migrate a single file from src to dst

substitutions is an optional dict of {regex: replacement} for performing replacements on the file.

functions.name: jupyter_core.migrate.migrate_one
documentation:
Migrate one item

dispatches to migrate_dir/_file

functions.name: jupyter_core.migrate.migrate_static_custom
documentation:
Migrate non-empty custom.js,css from src to dst

src, dst are 'custom' directories containing custom.{js,css}

functions.name: jupyter_core.migrate.migrate_config
documentation:
Migrate a config file.

Includes substitutions for updated configurable names.

functions.name: jupyter_core.migrate.migrate
documentation: Migrate IPython configuration to Jupyter

methods.name: jupyter_core.application.JupyterApp.load_config_file
documentation:
Load the config file.

By default, errors in loading config are handled, and a warning
printed on screen. For testing, the suppress_errors option is set
to False, so errors will make tests fail.

modules.name: jupyter_core.command
documentation:
The root `jupyter` command.

This does nothing other than dispatch to subcommands or output path info.
PYTHON_ARGCOMPLETE_OK

functions.name: jupyter_core.command.jupyter_parser
documentation: Create a jupyter parser object.

functions.name: jupyter_core.command.list_subcommands
documentation:
List all jupyter subcommands

searches PATH for `jupyter-name`

Returns a list of jupyter's subcommand names, without the `jupyter-` prefix.
Nested children (e.g. jupyter-sub-subsub) are not included.

functions.name: jupyter_core.command._execvp
documentation:
execvp, except on Windows where it uses Popen

Python provides execvp on Windows, but its behavior is problematic (Python bug#9148).

functions.name: jupyter_core.command._jupyter_abspath
documentation:
This method get the abspath of a specified jupyter-subcommand with no
changes on ENV.

functions.name: jupyter_core.command._path_with_self
documentation:
Put `jupyter`'s dir at the front of PATH

Ensures that /path/to/jupyter subcommand
will do /path/to/jupyter-subcommand
even if /other/jupyter-subcommand is ahead of it on PATH

functions.name: jupyter_core.command._evaluate_argcomplete
documentation:
If argcomplete is enabled, trigger autocomplete or return current words

If the first word looks like a subcommand, return the current command
that is attempting to be completed so that the subcommand can evaluate it;
otherwise auto-complete using the main parser.

functions.name: jupyter_core.command.main
documentation: The command entry point.

methods.name: jupyter_core.command.JupyterParser.epilog
documentation:
Add subcommands to epilog on request

Avoids searching PATH for subcommands unless help output is requested.

methods.name: jupyter_core.command.JupyterParser.epilog
documentation: Ignore epilog set in Parser.__init__

methods.name: jupyter_core.command.JupyterParser.argcomplete

A Jupyter argument parser.

modules.name: jupyter_core.paths
documentation: Path utility functions.

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.envset
documentation:
Return the boolean value of a given environment variable.

An environment variable is considered set if it is assigned to a value
other than 'no', 'n', 'false', 'off', '0', or '0.0' (case insensitive)

If the environment variable is not defined, the default value is returned.

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.use_platform_dirs
documentation:
Determine if platformdirs should be used for system-specific paths.

We plan for this to default to False in jupyter_core version 5 and to True
in jupyter_core version 6.

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.get_home_dir
documentation: Get the real path of the home directory

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths._do_i_own
documentation: Return whether the current user owns the given path

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.prefer_environment_over_user
documentation: Determine if environment-level paths should take precedence over user-level paths.

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths._mkdtemp_once
documentation:
Make or reuse a temporary directory.

If this is called with the same name in the same process, it will return
the same directory.

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.jupyter_config_dir
documentation:
Get the Jupyter config directory for this platform and user.

Returns JUPYTER_CONFIG_DIR if defined, otherwise the appropriate
directory for the platform.

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.jupyter_data_dir
documentation:
Get the config directory for Jupyter data files for this platform and user.

These are non-transient, non-configuration files.

Returns JUPYTER_DATA_DIR if defined, else a platform-appropriate path.

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.jupyter_runtime_dir
documentation:
Return the runtime dir for transient jupyter files.

Returns JUPYTER_RUNTIME_DIR if defined.

The default is now (data_dir)/runtime on all platforms;
we no longer use XDG_RUNTIME_DIR after various problems.

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.jupyter_path
documentation:
Return a list of directories to search for data files

JUPYTER_PATH environment variable has highest priority.

If the JUPYTER_PREFER_ENV_PATH environment variable is set, the environment-level
directories will have priority over user-level directories.

If the Python site.ENABLE_USER_SITE variable is True, we also add the
appropriate Python user site subdirectory to the user-level directories.

If `*subdirs` are given, that subdirectory will be added to each element.

Examples:

```pycon
>>> jupyter_path()
['~/.local/jupyter', '/usr/local/share/jupyter']
>>> jupyter_path('kernels')
['~/.local/jupyter/kernels', '/usr/local/share/jupyter/kernels']
```

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.jupyter_config_path
documentation:
Return the search path for Jupyter config files as a list.

If the JUPYTER_PREFER_ENV_PATH environment variable is set, the
environment-level directories will have priority over user-level
directories.

If the Python site.ENABLE_USER_SITE variable is True, we also add the
appropriate Python user site subdirectory to the user-level directories.

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.exists
documentation:
Replacement for `os.path.exists` which works for host mapped volumes
on Windows containers

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.is_file_hidden_win
documentation:
Is a file hidden?

This only checks the file itself; it should be called in combination with
checking the directory containing the file.

Use is_hidden() instead to check the file and its parent directories.

Parameters
----------
abs_path : unicode
    The absolute path to check.
stat_res : os.stat_result, optional
    The result of calling stat() on abs_path. If not passed, this function
    will call stat() internally.

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.is_file_hidden_posix
documentation:
Is a file hidden?

This only checks the file itself; it should be called in combination with
checking the directory containing the file.

Use is_hidden() instead to check the file and its parent directories.

Parameters
----------
abs_path : unicode
    The absolute path to check.
stat_res : os.stat_result, optional
    The result of calling stat() on abs_path. If not passed, this function
    will call stat() internally.

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.is_hidden
documentation:
Is a file hidden or contained in a hidden directory?

This will start with the rightmost path element and work backwards to the
given root to see if a path is hidden or in a hidden directory. Hidden is
determined by either name starting with '.' or the UF_HIDDEN flag as
reported by stat.

If abs_path is the same directory as abs_root, it will be visible even if
that is a hidden folder. This only checks the visibility of files
and directories *within* abs_root.

Parameters
----------
abs_path : unicode
    The absolute path to check for hidden directories.
abs_root : unicode
    The absolute path of the root directory in which hidden directories
    should be checked for.

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.win32_restrict_file_to_user
documentation:
Secure a windows file to read-only access for the user.
Follows guidance from win32 library creator:
http://timgolden.me.uk/python/win32_how_do_i/add-security-to-a-file.html

This method should be executed against an already generated file which
has no secrets written to it yet.

Parameters
----------

fname : unicode
    The path to the file to secure

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths._win32_restrict_file_to_user_ctypes
documentation:
Secure a windows file to read-only access for the user.

Follows guidance from win32 library creator:
http://timgolden.me.uk/python/win32_how_do_i/add-security-to-a-file.html

This method should be executed against an already generated file which
has no secrets written to it yet.

Parameters
----------

fname : unicode
    The path to the file to secure

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.get_file_mode
documentation:
Retrieves the file mode corresponding to fname in a filesystem-tolerant manner.

Parameters
----------

fname : unicode
    The path to the file to get mode from

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.secure_write
documentation:
Opens a file in the most restricted pattern available for
writing content. This limits the file mode to `0o0600` and yields
the resulting opened filed handle.

Parameters
----------

fname : unicode
    The path to the file to write

binary: boolean
    Indicates that the file is binary

functions.name: jupyter_core.paths.issue_insecure_write_warning
documentation: Issue an insecure write warning.
